planting.note: Grow as a houseplant or shaded patio container plant; Watermelon Peperomia is strictly a zone 10-12 outdoor plant and cannot survive frost. In zone 9, grow in containers that can be moved indoors when temperatures drop below 50°F. Plant in a small pot — Peperomia prefers to be slightly root-bound and is prone to root rot if overwatered in too large a container. Propagate by leaf cuttings: detach a leaf with stem, insert the cut end in moist perlite, and new plants emerge from the stem within 4–6 weeks.

directions: Grow in bright indirect to moderate shade — Watermelon Peperomia cannot tolerate direct sun (leaves bleach and scorch) and thrives in the lower light levels of north-facing windows, office interiors, and deeply shaded patios. Peperomia argyreia is a compact, rosette-forming tropical from South America (Brazil, Bolivia, Venezuela) prized for its extraordinarily decorative foliage: the thick, succulent, shield-shaped, dark olive-green leaves are marked with precisely parallel silver-gray stripes running from the center stem to the leaf edge in a pattern that mimics exactly the markings on a watermelon rind — an exceptional example of natural mimicry that makes the plant immediately recognizable and beloved. Each leaf is also subtly satin-textured, adding another dimension to the ornamental effect. The plant stays small and compact (under 12 inches) indefinitely, making it ideal for small pots, terrariums, dish gardens, and desktop displays. Watering is the key cultural challenge: Watermelon Peperomia has succulent stems and leaves that store considerable water, and overwatering causes rapid root rot — allow the top inch or two of soil to dry completely between waterings. Like all Peperomias, it is non-toxic to pets and children.
sources: Royal Horticultural Society — Peperomia argyreia cultivation
University of Florida IFAS Extension — Peperomia species for indoor use
Texas A&M AgriLife Extension — Peperomia for shade container gardens
American Society for the Prevention of Cruelty to Animals — Peperomia (non-toxic)

common_name: Emerald Ripple Peperomia (Ripple Peperomia, Green Ripple Pepper)

planting.note: Grow as a houseplant or shaded container plant in zone 9; Emerald Ripple Peperomia is not frost-tolerant and must be grown in containers brought indoors when temperatures approach 50°F. It tolerates low light better than most ornamental foliage plants and is an excellent choice for dim indoor spaces. Small pot size is important — root rot from overwatering in oversized pots is the most common failure. Allow soil to dry partially between waterings. Variegated forms ('Variegata', 'Silver Ripple') are equally easy to grow.

directions: Grow in bright indirect to low light in well-drained, peat-based or peat-free potting mix; Emerald Ripple Peperomia is one of the most tolerant of all foliage houseplants for low-light conditions, surviving in dim offices, north windows, and interior rooms that are challenging for most ornamentals. Peperomia caperata is a small, compact, rosette-forming tropical from Brazil in the pepper family (Piperaceae), prized for its deeply puckered, corrugated, heart-shaped leaves whose heavily textured surface creates a striking contrast of dark emerald green in the ridges and pale silver-green in the valleys — a tactile as well as visual interest that makes it one of the most distinctive small foliage plants available. The plant stays small (under 10 inches) indefinitely, making it ideal for terrariums, dish gardens, windowsill collections, and small decorative containers. In late summer and fall, the plant produces interesting, narrow, white rat-tail-like flower spikes — unusual in appearance but not particularly showy. Numerous cultivars exist including 'Rosso' (dark green above, intense red-purple below), 'Variegata' (cream-edged leaves), and 'Silver Ripple' (silver-gray textured leaves). All are easy to grow and safe for pets and children.
sources: Royal Horticultural Society — Peperomia caperata (AGM plant) cultivation
University of Florida IFAS Extension — Peperomia species for indoor culture
American Horticulture Society — Peperomia caperata indoor care
ASPCA Animal Poison Control — Peperomia species (non-toxic)

common_name: Crown of Thorns (Christ Plant, Christ Thorn, Euphorbia milii)

planting.note: Plant container-grown specimens in late spring in full sun in well-drained soil; in zone 9a, grow as a container plant to protect from frost. In zone 10-11, plant outdoors in a sunny, well-drained location — Crown of Thorns makes an excellent low hedge or specimen plant in frost-free climates. Water carefully: too much water causes root rot, too little causes leaf drop. The thick, milky latex sap is a severe skin and eye irritant — wear gloves when handling. Seeds are also toxic if ingested.

directions: Grow in full sun in very well-drained, gritty or sandy soil or potting mix; Crown of Thorns is extremely drought-tolerant and will rot quickly in wet, heavy soils or from overwatering. Euphorbia milii is a semi-succulent shrub native to Madagascar, heavily armed with long, sharp spines on the zigzagging, grayish stems — a characteristic thought to have inspired its Biblical associations with the Crown of Thorns worn by Jesus at the Crucifixion. Despite its formidable armament, Crown of Thorns is one of the most popular and widely cultivated succulent ornamentals in the world, particularly in tropical and subtropical regions where it is grown as an outdoor hedge, container specimen, and cut flower plant. The true flowers are tiny and inconspicuous, but they are surrounded by brilliantly colored, petal-like bracts in vivid scarlet, pink, orange, yellow, salmon, cream, or bicolor combinations — and the plant blooms nearly continuously in warm climates, making it one of the longest-blooming ornamentals available. Modern Thai hybrids (often marketed as 'Giant Euphorbia') produce much larger, showier bracts than the original species and are available in an extraordinary range of colors. CAUTION: The milky white latex sap is highly irritating to skin and eyes and toxic if ingested by humans or pets.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Euphorbia milii (Crown of Thorns)
Texas A&M AgriLife Extension — Euphorbia species for Gulf Coast landscapes
Floridata Plant Encyclopedia — Euphorbia milii
ASPCA Animal Poison Control — Euphorbia milii (toxic to pets)

planting.note: Plant divisions or small pots in spring in partial to full shade in consistently moist, well-drained soil; Baby Tears spreads rapidly by creeping stems to form a dense carpet and can become invasive in moist, shaded garden beds in zone 9. In zone 9a, it may be killed back by hard frost but resprouts from the roots in spring; in zone 9b-10 it is evergreen year-round. Excellent for filling between stepping stones in shaded paths, in terrariums, and as a living groundcover in containers beneath shade trees.

directions: Plant in partial to full shade in consistently moist, well-drained, humus-rich soil; Baby Tears cannot tolerate drought and will brown and die rapidly if the soil dries out, but equally cannot tolerate waterlogged conditions — the ideal is consistently moist soil that never dries or stands in water. Soleirolia soleirolii is a tiny, delicate, creeping perennial native to the islands of Corsica, Sardinia, and surrounding western Mediterranean, producing a dense, bright green to chartreuse, mossy carpet of minute, round, 1/4-inch leaves on thread-like, branching stems that root at every node. The effect of a well-established planting is an unbroken, emerald velvet carpet that perfectly covers soil, fills gaps between paving stones, and softens harsh edges in shaded garden spaces. The plant spreads steadily by its rooting stems to fill available space; once established in suitable moist, shaded conditions it requires no care other than occasional trimming at the edges. Baby Tears is one of the finest plants for creating a fresh, lush, mossy effect in shaded terrarium and vivarium plantings where it thrives in the humid enclosure. Also available in gold ('Aurea') and silver variegated ('Variegata') forms.
sources: Royal Horticultural Society — Soleirolia soleirolii cultivation notes
University of Florida IFAS Extension — Ground covers for shaded Florida landscapes
Texas A&M AgriLife Extension — Shade groundcovers for humid Gulf Coast
LSU AgCenter — Low-growing shade plants for Louisiana gardens

common_name: Coral Plant (Coral Physic Nut, Multifid Jatropha, Physic Nut)

planting.note: Plant container-grown shrubs after last frost in full sun with well-drained soil; Coral Plant grows rapidly in warm conditions and can reach 10 feet in zone 9-10. In zone 9a, it freezes to the ground or roots in cold winters but resprouts vigorously in spring; in zone 9b-10 it is a reliable semi-evergreen shrub. CAUTION: All parts of the plant are highly toxic — the seeds in particular contain curcin and ricin-like toxins and are potentially lethal if consumed by children or pets. The milky sap is also irritating.

directions: Plant in full sun in well-drained, sandy or rocky soil; Coral Plant is drought-tolerant and thrives in the poor, well-drained soils of zone 9 Gulf Coast landscapes, tolerating the summer heat and periodic drought that challenges many tropical ornamentals. Jatropha multifida is a large, architectural shrub or small tree from tropical America (Mexico, Central America, and the Caribbean) grown throughout the subtropical world for its striking combination of deeply lobed, palm-like leaves and vivid coral-red to scarlet flowers. The foliage is dramatically ornamental: large (up to 15 inches across), deeply cut into 7–11 finger-like lobes reminiscent of a Cannabis or Ricinus leaf, on long, flexible petioles that give the plant a bold, tropical sculptural quality. The flowers are borne in flat-topped clusters of tiny, brilliant coral-red, star-shaped blooms that appear prolifically at the branch tips throughout the warm season, attracting butterflies and occasionally hummingbirds. The interesting three-lobed fruit capsules that follow contain large seeds that, while potentially useful as a biofuel crop in some tropical production systems, are highly toxic and should not be consumed. In zone 9, Coral Plant is a fast-growing tropical accent that provides months of flowering interest and bold architectural foliage through the summer and fall.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Jatropha multifida (Coral Plant)
Floridata Plant Encyclopedia — Jatropha multifida
Texas A&M AgriLife Extension — Jatropha species for Gulf Coast landscapes
ASPCA Animal Poison Control Center — Jatropha species toxicity
